title: LifeGenIP Challenge - Unlearnable Videos against Diffusion-based Customization @ ECCV 2026
terms:
# Terms and Conditions

By registering for this competition you confirm that you have read, understood and will comply with the terms below.

## 1. Dataset Use

The competition uses the **[MEAD dataset](https://wywu.github.io/projects/MEAD/MEAD.html)** (Wang et al., ECCV 2020) with the permission of the MEAD authors. MEAD is released for **non-commercial, non-profit academic research only**. By registering for this competition you agree to:

- Comply with the **[MEAD Terms of Use](https://wywu.github.io/projects/MEAD/MEAD.html)**.
- Cite MEAD in any report, publication, presentation, or other material derived from your participation.
- **Not distribute, copy, reproduce, disclose, assign, sublicense, embed, host, transfer, sell, trade, or resell** any portion of MEAD to any third party for any purpose.
- **Not use MEAD, or any content derived from MEAD, in a defamatory, pornographic, or otherwise unlawful manner, or in violation of any applicable regulations or laws.** In particular, do not use any generative model to produce harmful, misleading, or identity-impersonating content of the individuals depicted in MEAD.

## 2. Prohibited Generative Use

You must not use the data, derived data, or any model trained on them to:

- Generate, publish, or disseminate content that **impersonates** any individual appearing in the dataset, or that misrepresents, defames, sexualizes, harasses, or otherwise harms them.
- Train or evaluate systems intended to **bypass privacy protection**, defeat anonymization, recover identity, perform face recognition or re-identification, or otherwise infer the real identity of any participant.
- Build commercial products, deploy commercial services, or perform identity-impersonation, surveillance, profiling, or any decision-making about individuals.

## 3. Submissions and Derived Data

- Submissions are used **solely for evaluation, leaderboard ranking and competition reporting**, including aggregate statistics and qualitative comparisons in the workshop write-up.
- You must not publicly release model weights, datasets, or other derived artifacts that could be used to reconstruct, identify, or impersonate any individual in MEAD.

## 4. Eligibility and Fair Play

- Open to academic and industry research teams. Each participant must register with their **real identity and institutional email**.
- One account per person; teams must declare members during registration.
- Do not probe the hidden test set beyond ordinary submissions, share accounts, or exploit infrastructure.

## 5. Liability

The data and evaluation infrastructure are provided **"as is"**, without warranty of any kind. Participants are responsible for the legality, ethics, and security of their own research activities, and shall defend and indemnify the organizers, the MEAD authors, and their affiliated institutions against any claims arising from their use of MEAD or competition data. The organizers reserve the right to revoke any participant's access to the data, or to disqualify any participant or submission at any time, including for violations of data privacy, submission rules, or Codabench platform rules.

## 6. Contact

Questions, requests for clarification, or reports of misuse: **lifegenip_workshop@googlegroups.com**

pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
# Overview

Official competition page: <https://lifegenip.cc/competition>

🔥 **Competition will begin on June 20th, and the starting kit and registration form will be released on June 15th.**

## Background

With recent advances in diffusion-based video generation, anyone can now synthesize high-quality video content from minimal input. This creates serious threats to facial privacy: a malicious attacker can collect publicly available clips of a target person and generate misleading or harmful content about them, from fake news to violations of portrait rights. Protecting video content from such misuse has become a pressing concern.

Current video customization tools follow two paradigms. **Tuning-based methods** fine-tune a video diffusion model (e.g., via LoRA) on a small set of clips of the target person and then generate diverse new videos from text prompts. **Reference-based methods**, such as image-to-video (I2V) models, take a single reference image plus a text prompt and synthesize a clip. Both pathways enable identity misuse. However, video-level protection has received much less attention than image protection. Defenses against tuning-based customization of video diffusion models remain largely unexplored, while existing work mostly targets image-domain editing or reference-based I2V threats.

## Task

Participants design a protection method that perturbs face-centric video clips. Protection succeeds when the customized output either does not reproduce the target identity or does not follow the attacker's text prompt.

## Threat Model

Two attack pathways:

- **Reference-based (I2V).** The attacker samples a single random frame from a protected clip and feeds it together with a text prompt to an I2V model to synthesize a short video. The attack succeeds if the generated video reproduces the target identity and follows the prompt.
- **Tuning-based.** The attacker uses the entire set of protected clips for one identity to fine-tune a video diffusion model via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). The customized model is then prompted to generate new videos. The attack succeeds if the generated videos reproduce the target identity and follow the prompts.

## Evaluation Tracks

- **Effectiveness** measures how strongly the protection breaks customization on the known model. Each protected clip is fed directly into the customization pipeline against the known model.
- **Robustness** measures whether the protection survives attacks that exploit inter-frame relationships. The protected video is first passed through a hidden temporal attack (e.g., temporal averaging) before being used for customization against the known model.
- **Transferability** measures whether the protection generalizes beyond the known model. Submissions are evaluated against hidden video diffusion models.

## Phases and Tracks

**Phase 1 — Preliminary** (open to all registered teams)

- Attack setting: reference-based (I2V).
- Tracks: **Effectiveness** and **Robustness**.

**Phase 2 — Final** (top teams from Phase 1)

- Attack setting: reference-based (I2V) + tuning-based (LoRA).
- Tracks: **Effectiveness**, **Robustness** and **Transferability**.

The final ranking is determined by Phase 2.

pages.title: Participation
pages.content:
# Participation

## Teams

- **Each team must create and use exactly one Codabench account** for registration and submission.
- Teams may have up to **10 members**.
- Participants may not use multiple accounts to evade submission limits, and may not be on more than one team.

## Hardware

A single NVIDIA GPU with **≥ 24 GB VRAM** is recommended (RTX 3090, RTX 4090, A5000, A6000). Tested on RTX 4090.

pages.title: Timeline
pages.content:
# Timeline

Phase 1 is open to all registered teams; Phase 2 is open only to top teams from Phase 1.

## Phase 1

- **Jun 15, 2026** — Phase 1 release
- **Jun 20, 2026** — Phase 1 evaluation opens
- **Jul 15, 2026** — Phase 1 evaluation closes

## Phase 2

- **Jul 15, 2026** — Phase 2 release
- **Jul 20, 2026** — Phase 2 evaluation opens
- **Jul 30, 2026** — Phase 2 evaluation closes

## Results

- **Aug 1, 2026** — Notification of final ranking
- **ECCV 2026** — Results announced at the LifeGenIP Workshop, Malmö, Sweden

All deadlines are end-of-day **Anywhere on Earth (AoE)**.

pages.title: Organizers
pages.content:
# Organizers

This competition is part of the [LifeGenIP Workshop](https://lifegenip.cc/) at **ECCV 2026** (Malmö, Sweden).

## Workshop Organizers

- [Ziming Hong](https://sites.google.com/view/ziminghong/), Sydney AI Centre, The University of Sydney
- [Runqi Lin](https://runqilin.github.io/), University of Oxford
- [Yongli Xiang](https://yonglixiang.github.io/), Sydney AI Centre, The University of Sydney
- [Jiaxin Huang](https://huangjiaxin.mystrikingly.com/), Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
- [Nils Lukas](https://nilslukas.github.io/), Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
- [Mingming Gong](https://mingming-gong.github.io/), The University of Melbourne; Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
- [Adel Bibi](https://www.adelbibi.com/), University of Oxford
- [Chris Russell](https://www.oii.ox.ac.uk/people/profiles/chris-russell/), University of Oxford
- [Philip Torr](https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~phst/), University of Oxford
- [Tongliang Liu](https://tongliang-liu.github.io/), Sydney AI Centre, The University of Sydney; Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

## Competition Organizers

- [Yuxin Huang](https://saythe17.github.io/), Sydney AI Centre, The University of Sydney
- [Lixu Wang](https://conditionwang.github.io/), The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen)
- [Shanshan Ye](https://cassie133ye.github.io/), Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
- [Gaoge Han](https://gaogehan.github.io/), Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

## Contact

For all competition matters please contact: lifegenip_workshop@googlegroups.com
